title: Destruction of the Second Temple
description: Roman forces under Titus destroy the Second Temple in Jerusalem, marking the beginning of the Jewish diaspora from Judea.

title: Bar Kokhba Revolt
description: After the failed revolt, Emperor Hadrian expels Jews from Jerusalem and renames the province Syria Palaestina. Approximately 580,000 Jews killed.

title: Sasanian Empire rises
description: Under Sasanian rule, Babylonian Jewry flourishes. The great academies of Sura and Pumbedita produce the Babylonian Talmud.

title: Rise of Islam
description: Jewish communities across the Middle East and North Africa come under Islamic rule. Generally tolerated as dhimmis, many communities thrive as merchants and scholars.

title: Moorish conquest of Iberia
description: Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula begins a golden age for Sephardic Jewry under relatively tolerant Islamic rule.

title: First Crusade pogroms
description: Crusader mobs massacre Jewish communities in the Rhineland (Speyer, Worms, Mainz, Cologne). Thousands killed. Many Jews flee eastward.

title: First blood libel (Norwich)
description: The first recorded blood libel accusation against Jews occurs in Norwich, England, setting a pattern of false accusations across medieval Europe.

title: Expulsion from France (Philip II)
description: Philip II expels Jews from royal French domains and confiscates their property. They are readmitted in 1198.

title: Expulsion from England
description: Edward I expels all Jews from England via the Edict of Expulsion. Jews would not officially return until 1657.

title: Expulsion from France
description: Philip IV expels all Jews from France and confiscates their property. Multiple cycles of expulsion and readmission follow.

title: Black Death persecutions
description: Jews are blamed for the Black Death across Europe. Hundreds of communities destroyed, thousands murdered. Major massacres in Strasbourg, Basel, and throughout the Rhineland.

title: Final expulsion from France
description: Charles VI permanently expels Jews from France. Many migrate to the Low Countries, Italy, and Eastern Europe.

title: Expulsion from Spain
description: The Alhambra Decree orders all Jews to convert or leave Spain. An estimated 100,000–200,000 Jews expelled. Sephardic diaspora spreads to the Ottoman Empire, North Africa, and the Netherlands.

title: Expulsion from Portugal
description: King Manuel I orders Jews to convert or leave Portugal, largely to satisfy conditions of his marriage alliance with Spain. Most are forcibly converted rather than expelled.

title: Venice Ghetto established
description: The Republic of Venice confines Jews to the Ghetto Nuovo, creating the first formal Jewish ghetto in Europe. The model spreads across the continent.

title: Papal expulsion from most of Italy
description: Pope Pius V expels Jews from the Papal States except Rome and Ancona, pushing many toward Eastern Europe and the Ottoman Empire.

title: Khmelnytsky massacres
description: The Cossack uprising led by Bohdan Khmelnytsky devastates Jewish communities in Ukraine and Poland. Estimated 100,000 Jews killed, hundreds of communities destroyed.

title: Jews arrive in New Amsterdam
description: A group of 23 Sephardic Jews arrives in New Amsterdam (later New York), establishing the first Jewish community in North America.

title: Golden age of Polish-Lithuanian Jewry
description: Despite periodic violence, the Jewish population of Poland-Lithuania grows to become the largest Jewish community in the world, centered around yeshivot and Hasidic movements.

title: First Partition of Poland
description: The partitions of Poland (1772, 1793, 1795) divide the largest Jewish community among Russia, Prussia, and Austria. Russia inherits millions of Jews it never wanted.

title: Pale of Settlement established
description: Catherine the Great confines Jews to the Pale of Settlement — western regions of the Russian Empire (modern Lithuania, Belarus, Ukraine, Moldova, and eastern Poland).

title: French emancipation
description: Revolutionary France becomes the first European country to grant full citizenship to Jews, beginning the era of Jewish emancipation in Western Europe.

title: Russian pogroms begin
description: Following the assassination of Tsar Alexander II, waves of anti-Jewish violence sweep southern Russia and Ukraine. Triggers mass emigration — over 2 million Jews leave the Russian Empire by 1914, mostly to the United States.

title: Dreyfus Affair
description: French-Jewish officer Alfred Dreyfus is falsely convicted of treason, exposing deep antisemitism in Western Europe. Theodor Herzl, covering the trial, is inspired to launch political Zionism.

title: First Zionist Congress
description: Theodor Herzl convenes the First Zionist Congress in Basel, Switzerland, founding the World Zionist Organization and declaring the goal of a Jewish homeland in Palestine.

title: Kishinev pogrom
description: A devastating pogrom in Kishinev (modern Chișinău, Moldova) kills 49 Jews and injures hundreds. Galvanizes international Jewish organizations and accelerates emigration.

title: Balfour Declaration
description: The British government declares support for 'the establishment in Palestine of a national home for the Jewish people,' shaping the future of Jewish migration to the region.

title: Immigration quotas in the US
description: The Emergency Quota Act (1921) and Johnson-Reed Act (1924) severely restrict immigration to the US, closing the primary escape route for European Jews.

title: Hitler comes to power
description: Adolf Hitler becomes Chancellor of Germany. Anti-Jewish legislation begins immediately, including boycotts of Jewish businesses and the first concentration camps.

title: Nuremberg Laws
description: Nazi Germany enacts the Nuremberg Laws, stripping Jews of German citizenship and banning marriages between Jews and non-Jews.

title: Kristallnacht
description: A coordinated pogrom across Nazi Germany and Austria. Synagogues burned, Jewish businesses destroyed, ~30,000 Jewish men sent to concentration camps. Marks the escalation to open violence.

title: World War II begins
description: Germany invades Poland. World Jewish population reaches its all-time peak of approximately 16.6 million on the eve of the Holocaust.

title: The Holocaust begins
description: Mass shootings by Einsatzgruppen in the Soviet Union, followed by systematic deportations to extermination camps. By 1945, approximately 6 million Jews are murdered.

title: End of World War II
description: Liberation of concentration camps. World Jewish population reduced to approximately 11 million. Survivors seek refuge in displaced persons camps, Palestine, and the Americas.

title: Establishment of Israel
description: The State of Israel is declared. Mass immigration (aliyah) begins — over 700,000 Jews arrive in the first three years, including Holocaust survivors and Jews from Arab countries.

title: Mizrahi exodus begins
description: Jews are expelled from or flee Arab and Muslim-majority countries. Over the next two decades, approximately 850,000 Mizrahi and Sephardic Jews leave Iraq, Yemen, Egypt, Morocco, Tunisia, Libya, and other countries — most resettling in Israel.

title: Suez Crisis — Egyptian Jews expelled
description: Following the Suez Crisis, Egypt expels most of its remaining Jewish population and confiscates their property.

title: Six-Day War
description: Israel captures the West Bank, Gaza, Sinai, and Golan Heights. Remaining Jews in several Arab countries face reprisals; most of Libya's Jews expelled.

title: Soviet Jewish emigration
description: The fall of the Iron Curtain enables mass emigration of Soviet Jews. Over 1 million emigrate to Israel in the following decade; hundreds of thousands go to the US and Germany.

title: Rising antisemitism in Europe
description: A wave of antisemitic attacks across Western Europe (Paris, Copenhagen, Brussels) accelerates Jewish emigration from France and other European countries to Israel.
